sentences.text: My name is Pearl Barach-- B-A-R-A-C-- like in 'candy'-- H.
My name-- my maiden name is Spiegelstein-- S-P-I-E-G-E-L-S-T-E-I-N.
December the 15th, 1923.
I was born in-- in Wohyn, Poland-- Wohyn-- W-O-H-Y-N.
My father's name was Jacob-- Jacob [?
Isaac, ?] and he was a wealthy man.
He was a good business man, and he did everything to make a nice living for us.
Yeah, he had rented a mill-- one to make flour with three more partners.
For-- and he-- and he sold it to wholesale places-- to bakeries and stores.
He made flour.
Not during the day, but he came home early, like 3 o'clock, 4 o'clock sometimes.
And he spent a lot of time with the children.
My mother's name was Braina Ruchel Nissenbaum-- N-I-S-E-N-B-A-U-M.
My mother was a good person.
She was beautiful-- blonde, blue eyes.
And she was a good housekeeper.
She took good care of the children.
And she had a lot of friends, and she was religious.
My oldest brother was Abraham Chaim, and next to him was Eliezer.
And then I was the third, and I had a brother Joshua.
And the next was Moshe, and I had a little baby sister-- she was born in 1938-- Chana.
No, but they went to a cheder.
I went to public school, and I finished just before the war broke out.
And I went to Bais Yaakov School for Girls.
The public school, we studied English, math, writing, reading.
We had gym.
I-- sew-- we learned how to sew and geography, history-- everything, but you have to learn.
Yes, we did.
Very good.
They're very-- we were very good friends.
Yes, I remember one-- [?
Zosha, ?] and I can't remember too many.
Mostly were Gentiles.
And one Jewish teacher was [?
Regina. ?] I don't remember her name now.
Yeah.
Very good-- the same-- no different than the other-- the Gentiles.
Yes, I had a few friends from school, from the class.
No, I don't remember that, no.
Yes, he did.
He had a lot of friends, many friends.
It was about around 300 to 350-- between 300 and 400, I would say.
There was one big synagogue, and there was a little-- two shtiebels.
My family prayed on the holidays and on-- on Shabbos.
They prayed at my uncle's shtiebel.
He gave up a room-- a big room-- for the holidays and for Shabbos.
During the week, they went to the big shul.
Oh, in Bais Yaakov, we learned how to read Hebrew, write Jewish, and we used to go-- every Saturday, we had minyan for the girls only, and that's about it.
Friday, my mother used to get up very early.
She used to be challahs and cake, and she cooked-- made gefilte fish.
She cooked, and she did everything.
And we sat down at the dinners Friday night.
My mother lit the candles, and we had the-- my father made kiddish.
And we were singing and eating, and we enjoyed it.
Shabbos morning, I went to do the Bais Yaakov's school.
We had [BACKROUND NOISE] a minyan, we davened.
And--
In the afternoon, we played with the children, with the friends.
And--
Oh, Passover was a very big holiday.
We had to change the dishes and do a lot of shopping.
You used to bake matzos.
[INAUDIBLE].
They didn't buy a ready-made, but they used to go to a special bakery.
Everything was kosher.
And we had a lot to-- to prepare.
A little bit.
She'd send me places to-- to shop, you know, and to-- I helped a little, as much I could.
Oh, the Seder-- the whole family sat down, and they [INAUDIBLE].
And we drank the wine and gefilte fish.
And everything we ate, and we enjoyed it.
No, in Europe, everybody ate by himself.
They all had big families, and they ate-- in Europe wasn't like here.
Purim?
Oh, it was a very-- [SIGHS] we were fasting.
I mean, not me, but they were fasting.
And then they made a big seudah, and they baked and cooked, and it was a big holiday.
No, I don't remember that.
Well, Rosh Hashanah was a holiday, and we went to shul.
I did too.
And Yom Kippur, we fast.
[BACKROUND NOISE]
I lived on Rynek number 6-- it means Marketplace number 6.
And across the street was a church.
Oh, we live in a-- yes, my grandfather left an estate.
It was, like, four apartments.
My grandmother lived in one with her daughter, [?
Esther ?] Spiegelstein.
And then my-- we lived in the front and one side.
And then the other front apartment-- another-- another sister-- my father's-- with her husband and children.
Sarah Luba.
And in the back, my father's niece lived there with a family, with her family.
And we had a big yard.
And my uncle, Sarah Luba's husband, had [? a factory ?] [? from ?] soda and seltzer in the backyard of the house.
In my apartment, there were about three rooms.
But later on, when my brothers got bigger, they fixed-- on the-- on the top-- on the attic, they fixed two more bedrooms.
No.
No.
No.
Yes.
I-- I forgot.
They were two rabbis, and I don't remember the names.
Well, they were very religious with big beards, and they wore white socks and silk coats and a shtreimel.
Yes, always because they did business with the Jews.
And--
Not everybody, but some were well off, and some weren't.
The trouble first began right after they took over Poland.
They gave-- the Germans came in.
They gave orders to bring in all-- all the radios, the valuables, and the jewelry, and the fur coats-- everything.
And then after, they burned the whole town.
They threw firebombs from planes, and they burned the town.
Yes, I was in the center of town.
And when we heard the bombs, we were running out.
And right near my foot, a bomb fell, but I wasn't hurt.
And we ran out of town to the woods, to the farms.
No, nothing that I remember.
This happened right after they took over Poland.
Oh, in '39-- 1939 in September.
Yes.
Whatever-- my mother had jewelry and watches, bicycle, and what else-- a radio.
We had our radio with earphones, and everything in--
We went to a farm-- my father's friend [?
Benedict ?] Rycerski-- R-Y-C-E-R-S-K-I.
And we stayed there for a few weeks.
Then we came to town.
My-- the-- my father-- at-- he rented the mill from the people.
They gave-- they build a new house, so they gave us their old house, and we lived there.
It wasn't-- you know, not really in the-- in the center of the town, but it was a little further away.
Well, they all ran wherever they could-- wherever they could.
I don't know, but they went to the farms, to the-- there were no houses-- not too many were-- were left-- not too many Jewish houses.
In this house-- about a year.
My grandmother died there.
Matel Spiegelstein.
We have to wear our armband with a Jewish star-- a white armband with a blue Jewish star, and we could not walk all over.
We had to walk in a certain-- I mean, to-- in a certain place.
No, not no more-- no businesses.
No, he didn't.
I don't know.
They-- whatever they had, they could buy bread, and have it-- I think they-- whatever they had-- money left or whatever.
They all promised us, if [INAUDIBLE] then, they'll hide us.
The all promised because we heard what's going on, and we knew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got some food from the Gentiles too.
They were in the farms.
They had flour, you know, they-- whatever.
We-- we didn't do nothing.
We just got together with the people, you know, during the day.
And that's all.
At night, we weren't allowed to go out.
Yes, they met to daven.
All-- all-- only in the neighborhood.
There never was a room-- any room.
The shul was burned-- the shtiebel-- everything.
Next?
My father sent me away to a-- a village.
He knew the people, and I used to help in the kitchen.
Kopina-- K-O-P-I-N-A.
I helped out in the kitchen.
I peeled potatoes.
The men was working, I used-- were working-- and I used to bring out water for them to drink and food sometimes-- whatever they needed.
Jews separate, and Gentiles-- they were from before, but they were-- worked separate.
And Ukrainians were watching us, and there one from Estonia also watching us.
I would say about a year.
There were no Jews in this farm-- only the people who came from our town to work.
What happened-- one day-- I-- I didn't sleep in the kitchen where everybody slept.
I slept in an attic with my friend [INAUDIBLE].
And they start to gather-- gather the people to send out the people to the Ghetto Miedzyrzec.
And-- and they-- and a-- and a Gentile told them that we are upstairs in the attic sleeping.
And they got us too.
They took us out.
But and one man watched us-- me and my friend.
But we weren't with the other people yet, but they were gonna take us.
So I bribe-- I bribed him.
I wanted to bribe him.
I did.
I gave him my-- I had gold earrings, and I had some money.
And I gave him-- I said, let me run away.
Let me go-- me and my friend.
He-- he took everything, and then he pulled a gun, and he said, you're not going anyplace.
Then he-- I-- somehow, he left, and another one came.
I had no money.
I had nothing left.
But I said, [POLISH], please let me go.
Let us go.
They said, you go-- go, then we start.
So my friend said, where are you going?
He's going to kill you.
Why are you running?
I said, let's go.
Let's take a chance.
So we ran.
He didn't do nothing.
He let us-- he let us go.
So we ran in-- on-- went through the fields, and we went into a barn because we didn't want to walk during the day, and we stayed there.
We say other people passing with a horse and buggy.
You know, the people who they-- and they took them to Miedzyrzec.
And when it got dark, I tried-- we tried to get home to my-- to Wohyn.
I came home, and-- and my brother was looking for me.
They-- they passed our town.
They didn't see me.
And they were worried about me.
They didn't see me on the-- on the-- with the people.
But then I came home, and I-- they were afraid they gonna look for me-- they'll come to look for me because I was there, and I disappeared.
So they send me to this man again from the mill, and I was there-- I was hiding there for a few days in the attic.
But they didn't come to me.
Yeah, I went home.
In the house.
Yeah, with my brother.
This was the-- the older than me, not the oldest.
The oldest had a-- a-- he had a passport-- an Irish passport, and he went to [?
Warsaw. ?] And he-- like a Polack.
Yes.
Oh, they-- oh, they were-- they killed them two, because he-- he wasn't-- they were in a different farm, and they didn't work because they were too young yet.
But they slept in a-- also in an attic by a Gentile.
And one Gentile told the Germans that two Jews are sleeping there.
They took them out and killed them.
They were dead already.
Oh, yes, sure.
You could imagine, but it was-- they were very bad, very bad.
Yeah, they used to come in town and take out the Jews and put them in the mart and beat them, but not-- until then, not so many.
Only even the, you know, the Poles used to say-- they didn't know who was Jewish or not.
The Germans didn't know, but the Poles helped them a lot.
My baby sister-- they had to take-- they gave orders right in the beginning that they should take out the-- the small children to a town Parczew-- P-A-R-C-Z-E-W.
My-- my aunt-- my father's sister-- took-- went with her, and we never saw them again.
No.
No, nothing.
One day, the Germans came into town, they-- they-- and they start shooting around the houses, near the houses.
And I went into a corner.
I-- I covered myself with a-- with a basin-- my face.
They didn't see me, but I was in a corner sitting, but I still wanted to protect my head and face.
And my brother Eliezer-- he jumped out the window, and he was shot to death.
Soon after they left the same day.
They killed a lot of people.
They buried them.
We ran to look-- look for a place where to hide.
But when we thought this was going on, my father had a lot of friends.
They all promised him.
But when it came to-- they-- all they were afraid.
They didn't want to do it.
So we had to come back.
This was a small ghetto.
This was like in outskirts of the town.
It wasn't in the center.
But a few houses weren't-- weren't burned, so they let us stay there.
And we weren't allowed to go all over.
No, no.
Yes.
I don't know.
Not too many.
I don't know what happened to them.
They went, maybe, to different towns-- there wasn't much room there-- maybe to some Gentile people.
I-- I really don't know.
No.
Oh, they took some people to work, but they sent them to camps-- to concentration camps-- to working camps, maybe.
I don't know.
Next, they-- they-- we went to the-- they took us to the ghetto, to Miedzyrzec.
A-- a few hours with by horse and buggy.
Oh, the-- I don't remember how.
We went on a horse and buggy, but I don't know what they did-- they did, you know, if they hired-- they told-- they told people to get out of town to go to Miedzyrzec.
I guess, everybody hired a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a horse and buggy, and we went.
Not much, not much.
We didn't have much because we-- everything was burnt, and you could not buy.
There were no stores.
In this ghetto, at the end of the ghetto-- or the beginning-- there was a little house, and we stayed there.
And we paid somebody to build a double wall in case something will happen, we'll hide there.
OK, how did I do?
